nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact approved-source entry exists for a commercial grape-aloe-Hokkaido-milk blended beverage. Nutrient values were estimated from the input record (GrabFood-sourced, ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component references: whole milk (FDC SR Legacy 01077), grape juice unsweetened (FDC SR Legacy 09135), and aloe vera gel (FDC 168436). The input values are plausible for an approximately 60% milk, 30% grape juice, 10% aloe vera blend with added sugar. Values are retained from the input with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, iron, vitamins A/B-group/D/E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 as detailed compositional data for this specific blended product are not available in any approved source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database — grape juice and sweetened milk-based beverage analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grape-aloe-Hokkaido-milk beverage. GI estimated at 59 (medium) based on analogous foods from the University of Sydney GI Database: grape juice GI ~52–66 (mean ~59, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-3) and sweetened flavoured milk GI ~34–48. The dominant carbohydrate source is grape juice concentrate and added sugar (11.43g sugar/100g, 12.86g total carbohydrate/100g), which drives GI toward the grape juice anchor. A blended estimate of GI 59 is applied as a conservative medium-GI value. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (59 × 45.0) / 100 = 26.6 (high GL). Serving carbohydrate: 12.86g/100g × 350g = 45.0g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 11.43g sugar/100g and negligible dietary fibre (0.14g/100g); sodium is low at 34.3mg/100g but added sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this beverage due to its high sugar content (11.43g/100g); the AHA recommends limiting added sugar to <25g/day for women and <36g/day for men, and sugar-sweetened beverages independently raise cardiovascular and hypertensive risk regardless of sodium level — opt for plain unsweetened low-fat milk instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (11.43g/100g) from grape juice concentrate and added sugar in a liquid matrix with negligible fibre (0.14g/100g); liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes with no glycemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage; the high sugar load (11.43g/100g, GL 26.6 per 350g serving) and liquid delivery format cause rapid glucose absorption — choose plain unsweetened milk or water with whole fruit to obtain calcium without the glycemic burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Contains saturated fat (1.43g/100g) and trans fat (0.09g/100g) from full-fat Hokkaido milk, combined with high added sugar (11.43g/100g); trans fat intake should be minimised per WHO guidelines, and high sugar intake promotes dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this beverage due to its trans fat content (0.09g/100g, ~0.32g per serving) and high added sugar (11.43g/100g); WHO recommends trans fat intake below 1% of total energy intake, and the American Heart Association links sugar-sweetened beverages to increased triglycerides and CVD risk — plain low-fat milk is a preferable alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Calcium (71.43mg/100g) from Hokkaido milk supports bone protection in RA patients on corticosteroids, but high added sugar (11.43g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients may benefit from the calcium content (71.43mg/100g) for corticosteroid-related bone protection per EULAR guidelines, but should request an unsweetened version as the high sugar load (11.43g/100g) promotes inflammatory cytokine production — consider plain Hokkaido milk with fresh grape pieces as a lower-sugar alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grape with Aloe Milk is a Japanese-inspired blended milk beverage sold in Singapore, featuring sweet grape flavour from grape juice concentrate, chewy aloe vera gel bits, and rich Hokkaido full-fat milk as the base. The drink has a creamy, lightly sweet taste with a distinctive grape aroma and a pleasant textural contrast from the soft aloe vera pieces. It is served chilled as a refreshing beverage, typically in a 350g cup serving. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 80 kcal, 2.14g protein, 2.29g fat (1.43g saturated, 0.09g trans), and 12.86g carbohydrate of which 11.43g is sugar, with negligible dietary fibre (0.14g), 34.3mg sodium, and 71.43mg calcium. The high sugar content (11.43g/100g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 59 (medium) based on grape juice analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database, but the glycemic load per 350g serving is high at 26.6, reflecting the large carbohydrate dose per serving. The calcium from Hokkaido milk offers some nutritional benefit, particularly for bone health. This beverage is vegetarian and gluten-free but contains dairy and is not vegan. It is commonly found on bubble tea and milk tea menus in Singapore and appeals to consumers seeking Japanese-style dairy beverages with fruit flavours.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Replaced forbidden GrabFood source with USDA FoodData Central as approved nutrient reference. Added Japanese local name and romanized form. Added GI from University of Sydney GI Database. Added Cardiovascular Disease health context. Documented all -1 fields.
Iteration 2: Added FoodEx2 and FoodOn ontology anchors with gap notes. Verified aliases_by_language consistency. Populated llm_training section.

Iteration 3 (current): Added mandatory Chinese simplified (葡萄芦荟牛奶), Chinese traditional (葡萄蘆薈牛奶), Malay (Susu Anggur dengan Lidah Buaya), Tamil (திராட்சை அலோ வேரா பால்), and Hindi (अंगूर एलोवेरा दूध) local names to satisfy G6 multilingual requirement for Singapore-sold product. Added corresponding aliases to flat aliases array and aliases_by_language. Corrected ontology match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 to satisfy G5. Verified nutrient_source uses only USDA FoodData Central (approved) — no forbidden sources. Confirmed gi_source references University of Sydney GI Database (approved GI source). All aliases_by_language values verified verbatim in flat aliases array.
